content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 14, 2019

Richard Hoard
Trinity County
Planning Commissioner
Po Box 112
Weaverville CA 96048

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-19-196

Dear Mr. Hoard:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

Because your inquiry is general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

May you participate in decisions as a member of the Trinity County Planning Commission where a client of the local engineering consulting firm you are privately employed by as a structural engineer has a matter before the Planning Commission?

CONCLUSION

Generally, you have a potentially disqualifying interest in your employer as a business entity and source of income. Where the decisions will have a foreseeable and material financial effect on your employer under Regulation 18702.1(a)(2) or (a)(3), you may not take part in the decisions

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-19-196
Page No. 2

except under the limited exception for contacts and appearances under Regulation 18704(d)(6), as
discussed below. However, you have not identified any particular decision before your agency. For
general advice, as a planning commissioner, you must analyze whether each decision you
may have a foreseeable and material effect on your employer. In regard to decisions affecting a
client of your employer, whether you are disqualified will depend on the specific nature of each
decision including, but not limited to, your firm's involvement in the project, the likelihood the
project will result in additional work for your firm, and the nature and size of the project.
Accordingly, absent a specific decision before your agency, we can only state that you are
potentially disqualified from decisions implicating the client. We recommend that you seek
additional advice when you have facts regarding a particular decision.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You have been a County Planning Commissioner for almost 3 years. You recently started
working for a local private engineering consulting firm doing structural engineering work as a
salary employee. Being one of the only engineering companies in this rural area, the firm is
constantly sought for a wide array of projects that relate directly to Planning Commission matters,
such as: tentative map subdivisions, projects involving conditional use permits, and cannabis-
related projects. You have no ownership interest in the firm, nor are you advanced financially for
the success of any project.

You wish to know if you have a conflict of interest based on your employment on matters
that come before the Planning Commission that involve work you may perform for your firm, work
performed by others at your firm where you have no involvement, or involves a firm client that may
later come before the Planning Commission on an unrelated matter. For example, you wish to know
if you may have a conflict of interest where a given project may come to you to design some
infrastructure that will be used on a cannabis project, and the client seeks a variance related to the
project. Another scenario you provided is where you or another member or your firm prepared the
design and drawings for a subdivision project that comes before the Planning Commission.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on one or more of
the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) Section 87103 defines
financial interests to include, as relevant to these facts:

• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect
investment worth $2,000 or more; or is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(a) and
(d).)

File No. I-19-196
Page No. 3

• Any source of income, aggregating $500 or more in value provided or
promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the
time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).)

You have a business entity interest (including the business as a source of income) in your
employer. Therefore, you must recuse yourself from making, participating in making or influencing
a government decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on your
employer.

Reasonably Foreseeable and Material Financial Effect

Regulation 18701(a) provides that a decision's effect on an official's interest is presumed to
be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is explicitly involved in the decision as a named party in, or
the subject of the decision. An interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the interest.

Regulation 18701(b) sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable to a decision's effect
on an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision and provides that the
decision's effect on such an interest is reasonably foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a realistic
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical."

Your request presents situations where your employer's client is the named applicant or
subject of the decision, and not your employer. For this situation, your employer is not explicitly
involved in the decision, and the latter standard applies.

Materiality: Business Entity and Business as a Source of Income

Where you have an interest in your employer as a business entity and as a source of income,
we look to Regulation 18702.1, the materiality standard for a business, to determine the material
financial effect of a decision. (Regulation 18702.3(a)(4)).³ Relevant to your facts, Regulation
18702.1(a) provides that a decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an official's
business interest is material if any of the following criteria are met:

• The decision may result in an increase or decrease of the business's annual
gross revenues, or the value of the business's assets or liabilities, in an
amount equal to or more than $1,000,000; or five percent of the business's
annual gross revenues and at least $10,000. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(2).)

• The decision may cause the business to incur or avoid additional expenses,
or to reduce or eliminate expenses, in an amount equal to or more than

³ Under Regulation 18702.3(a)(4), the materiality standard for a source of income that is a business entity is
the same as for an interest in the business entity itself.

File No. 1-19-196
Page No. 4

$250,000; or one percent of the business's annual gross revenues and at
least $2,500. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(3).)

Where a client of your employer is involved in a decision before the Planning Commission,
you must determine if the decision may have a foreseeable and material financial impact on your
employer. The prohibition on your involvement is triggered by the impact on your employer and
applies whether or not you personally worked on the client's matter.

Making, Participating in, or Influencing a Decision

Where you determine that a decision will foreseeably impact your employer under the above
standards, you are prohibited from 'making, participating in or influencing that decision.'⁴ An
official "makes" a government decision if the official "authorizes or directs any action, votes,
appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any
contractual agreement on behalf of his agency." (Regulation 18704(a).) "Participating in" a
government decision occurs when the official "provides information, an opinion, or a
recommendation for the purpose of affecting the decision without significant intervening
substantive review." (Regulation 18704(b).) Lastly, a public official "uses his or her position to
influence a decision" if the official:

(1) Contacts or appears before any official in his or her agency or in an agency
subject to the authority or budgetary control of his or her agency for the purpose
of affecting a decision; or

(2) Contacts or appears before any official in any other government agency for the
purpose of affecting a decision, and the public official acts or purports to act
within his or her authority or on behalf of his or her agency in making the contact.

(Regulation 18704(c).)

The Planning Commission and its staff constitutes your "agency." As a practical matter, this
means that you must recuse yourself from any decision before the Planning Commission that
involves your employer's client and will foreseeably and materially impact your employer. You do
not provide information as to the staffing structure for the Planning Commission. However, we
caution that if the Planning Commission shares staff with the County, contact with shared County
staff may also amount to influencing one's own agency. (See e.g. Watson Advice letter, No. A-14-
104.)

Exception to Allow Limited Contact with Planning Commission Staff

⁴ When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 (including Planning Commissioners)
has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must immediately following the
announcement of the agenda item to be discussed or voted upon (1) publicly identify the type of financial interest that
has a conflict, and applicable to your interest, state the name of the business, your position and a general
description of the business' activity, (2) do so orally and as part of the official record, and (3) recuse himself or herself
and leave the room. (Regulation 18707.)

File No. 1-19-196
Page No. 5

Apart from this prohibition, there is a narrowly construed exception which allows an official
who is an engineer in his or her private capacity to make a limited appearance or contact on the
matter before his or her agency. The exception under Regulation 18704(d)(6) states that "making,
participating in or influencing a government decision" does not include:

(A) Drawings or submissions of an architectural, engineering, or similar nature
prepared by a public official for a client to submit in a proceeding before the
official's agency if;

(i) The work is performed pursuant to the official's profession; and

(ii) The official does not make any contact with the agency other than contact with
agency staff concerning the process or evaluation of the documents prepared by the
official.

Commission staff has narrowly construed this exception, finding that it is limited to the
preparation of technical documents and necessary contact with agency staff concerning the
processing or evaluation of the drawings or submissions prepared by the official. (Rumansoff
Advice Letter, No. 1-94-045.) "Necessary contact" is also narrowly construed to allow an official to
respond to questions from agency staff regarding the evaluation of drawings and submissions
prepared by the official or relating to their movement through the approval process. (Woody Advice
Letter, No. 1-99-061 and Holbert Advice Letter, No. 1-90-080.)

Contacts or Appearances: Other County Agencies

As a general matter, once the Planning Commission no longer has any jurisdiction over the
decision in which you have a financial interest, and it is before the County Board of Supervisors, or
other County agency that is not subject to the authority or budgetary control of the Planning
Commission, and does not share staff with the Planning Commission, you may participate in the
decision at this level, provided you are clear that you are acting in your private capacity.
(Regulation 18704(c)(2).) This means that you may not present yourself as acting for, or on behalf
of, the Planning Commission. We recommend that you seek further advice regarding your
participation in the event that you determine that you have a foreseeable and material financial
interest in a decision.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: L. Karen Harrison
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:aja

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. A public official has a ''.financial interest" in a governmental 
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a

• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect 
investment worth $2,000 or more; or is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(a) and 
(d).)  
• Any source of income, aggregating $500 or more in value provided or 
promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the 
time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
You have a business entity interest (including the business as a source of income) in your

gross revenues, or the value of the business's assets or liabilities, in an

annual gross revenues and at least $10,000. (Regulation 18702.l(a)(2).)

or to reduce or eliminate expenses, in an amount equal to or more than

the same as for an interest in the business entity itself.  
$250,000; or one percent of the business's annual gross revenues and at 
least $2,500. (Regulation 18702.l(a)(3).)
